beats.text: You've practiced equations with all kinds of groups and unknowns. Let's connect what you discovered.

params.description: Equation A displays: 3 × 5 = 15
Equation B displays: 15 = 3 × 5
Equation C displays: 3 × 5 = 20
Equation D displays: 15 = 5 × 3

beats.text: Four equations. Three are true, both sides have the same value. One is false. Which one is false?
Which equation is false?

validator.description: Student selected C (3 × 5 = 20), the false equation

params.description: Equation C highlights to confirm selection.

beats.text: Right. 3 times 5 is 15, not 20. The sides don't match.

validator.description: Student selected A (3 × 5 = 15), which is a true equation

params.description: Equation A highlights.

beats.text: 3 times 5 is 15. Both sides match, so this equation is true. We need the false one.

validator.description: Student selected B (15 = 3 × 5), which is a true equation

params.description: Equation B highlights.

beats.text: 15 equals 3 times 5. Both sides have the same value, so this is true. Look for the equation where the sides don't match.

validator.description: Student selected D (15 = 5 × 3), which is a true equation

params.description: Equation D highlights.

beats.text: 5 times 3 is 15. The sides match. A false equation has sides that don't equal each other. Which one?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Equation C highlights, showing the mismatch between 3 × 5 (15) and 20.

beats.text: Let me show you. 3 times 5 is 15, not 20. The left side gives us 15, but the right side says 20. They don't match, so this equation is false. That's equation C.
And notice, the other equations are all true. The total is on the right in one and on the left in others, but both sides have the same value either way. That's what the equals sign checks.

elements.description: Equation C: 3 × 5 = 20, highlighted as the false equation

params.description: Image 1: 4 rows of 2 books on shelves
Image 2: 4 stacks of 2 cups
Image 3: 4 groups of 2 butterflies (circled clusters)
Equation displayed below all three images: 4 × 2 = 8

beats.text: Three pictures. One equation fits all of them. What makes them the same?
What do all three pictures have in common?

validator.description: Student selected A and B only, both true

beats.text: Both true! They all have 8 things, and the reason is the same every time: 4 groups of 2. The structure underneath is what the equation captures.

validator.description: Branch 2: Student selected only one correct answer (either A or B), not both

params.description: Equation segments 4, 2, and 8 highlight.

beats.text: That's true. Look at the equation again. What else does it tell us about the pictures? There's one more answer that fits.

validator.description: Branch 3: Student selected only incorrect answers (C and/or D)

params.description: Books image highlights the 4 groups of 2 structure.
Cups image highlights the 4 groups of 2 structure.
Butterflies image highlights the 4 groups of 2 structure.

beats.text: The pictures look different, but think about the structure. How many groups? How many in each group? That's what makes them fit the same equation.

validator.description: Branch 4: Student selected at least one correct and at least one incorrect answer

params.description: Books image border highlights.
Cups image border highlights.
Butterflies image border highlights.

beats.text: You found something that's true about all three. But look closely at the pictures. One of your other choices doesn't fit. Books, cups, and butterflies are not the same thing.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Books: groups count from 1 to 4, then items in each group count to 2.
Cups: groups count from 1 to 4, then items in each group count to 2.
Butterflies: groups count from 1 to 4, then items in each group count to 2.

beats.text: Let me show you. Books: 4 groups of 2. Cups: 4 groups of 2. Butterflies: 4 groups of 2. That's the structure. When we count, we get 8 things every time. The answers are 'All have 4 groups of 2' and 'All have 8 things.' The equation captures that structure, no matter what the objects are.

elements.description: Image 1: 4 rows of 2 books on shelves
Equation displayed below all three images: 4 × 2 = 8

params.description: First equation displays: 4 × 5 = ☐ (product unknown)
Second equation displays below first: 4 × ☐ = 20 (second factor unknown)
Third equation displays below second: ☐ × 5 = 20 (first factor unknown)

params.label: product unknown
params.description: Label appears next to first equation: product unknown

params.label: second factor unknown
params.description: Label appears next to second equation: second factor unknown

params.label: first factor unknown
params.description: Label appears next to third equation: first factor unknown

beats.text: Three equations. Same numbers: 4, 5, and 20. But the unknown moved to a different spot each time. What stays the same?
What stays the same in all three equations?
options: The unknown is always the hardest number
The numbers are different each time
The equation changes meaning each time

validator.description: Student selected option A: 4 groups of 5 equals 20

beats.text: Right. 4 groups of 5 equals 20. That relationship stays the same no matter where the unknown is.

condition.selected: The unknown is always the hardest number

validator.description: Student selected option B: The unknown is always the hardest number

params.description: All three numbers highlight across the equations

beats.text: The unknown changes position, but that does not change the numbers themselves. Look at what the numbers mean together. What relationship do 4, 5, and 20 show?

condition.selected: The numbers are different each time

validator.description: Student selected option C: The numbers are different each time

params.description: The numbers 4, 5, and 20 highlight across all three equations

beats.text: Look closely at the numbers. Each equation uses 4, 5, and 20. The unknown moves, but the numbers stay the same. What do those three numbers mean together?

condition.selected: The equation changes meaning each time

validator.description: Student selected option D: The equation changes meaning each time

params.description: The numbers 4, 5, and 20 highlight across all three equations

beats.text: The position of the unknown changes, but the meaning does not. All three equations show the same relationship. Read what 4, 5, and 20 mean together.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: The numbers 4, 5, and 20 pulse in sequence across all three equations, showing the consistent relationship

beats.text: Let me show you what stays the same. In every equation, 4 groups of 5 equals 20. That relationship does not change. The unknown moves to different positions, but the fact that 4 groups of 5 makes 20 is always true. The answer is: 4 groups of 5 equals 20.

elements.description: Second equation: 4 × ☐ = 20 with label 'second factor unknown'
elements.label: second factor unknown

elements.description: Third equation: ☐ × 5 = 20 with label 'first factor unknown'
elements.label: first factor unknown

beats.text: The unknown moves, but the relationship stays. 4 groups of 5 is always 20. You figure out whichever piece is missing.

elements.description: Second equation: 4 × ☐ = 20 with label 'second factor unknown'
elements.label: second factor unknown

elements.description: Third equation: ☐ × 5 = 20 with label 'first factor unknown'
elements.label: first factor unknown

params.label: Last time
params.description: Equation appears on left side of screen: 6 × 5 = 30, labeled 'Last time'. Standard form, no unknown, bags context image above.

params.label: Today
params.description: Equation appears on right side of screen: ☐ = 3 × 10, labeled 'Today'. Reversed form with unknown on left, stacks context image above.

beats.text: Here's an equation you have seen before. And here's one from today. What changed?
What's different about today's equation?
options: The total moved to the other side
The picture uses stacks, not bags
All of these are different today

condition.selected: All of these are different today

validator.description: Student selected D, recognizing all three M10 changes together

beats.text: Right. All of these are different today. Three new things, all in one equation. The equals sign works both ways. Unknowns can go anywhere. And equations work for stacks, rows, groups, not just bags.

condition.selected: The total moved to the other side

validator.description: Student selected A: focused only on the reversed equation structure

params.description: Unknown box highlights in today's equation.

beats.text: The total did move, but look closer. What's in the box? That's an unknown. And what picture do you see? That's a stack, not a bag. All three things changed.

validator.description: Student selected B: focused only on the unknown box

params.description: Equals sign highlights in last time's equation.
Equals sign highlights in today's equation.

beats.text: There is an unknown, but that's not the only change. Notice where the equals sign is. The total is on a different side. And the picture changed from bags to stacks.

condition.selected: The picture uses stacks, not bags

validator.description: Student selected C: focused only on the context image change

params.description: Unknown box and equals sign highlight in today's equation.

beats.text: The picture did change from bags to stacks. But look at the equation itself. Where's the total now? And what's in that box? Three things are different today.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Today's equation highlights all three changes: unknown box, reversed structure with total on left, and stacks context image.

beats.text: Let me show you all three changes. Last time, the total was on the right. Today it's on the left. Last time, every number was known. Today there's a box for an unknown. Last time we had bags. Today we have stacks. All of these are different today.

elements.description: Equation on left: 6 × 5 = 30, labeled 'Last time', with bags context image.
elements.label: Last time

elements.description: Equation on right: ☐ = 3 × 10, labeled 'Today', with stacks context image.
elements.label: Today

beats.text: If you had to remember one thing from today, what would it be?
What's the one thing to remember?
options: Different pictures can have the same equation

validator.description: Student selected option A: equals sign meaning

beats.text: That's it. Same value as. That's the equals sign's job. You'll use that in every equation from now on.

validator.description: Student selected option B: unknown position flexibility

beats.text: Right. Unknowns move, but the relationship stays. You can figure out any missing piece.

condition.selected: Different pictures can have the same equation

validator.description: Student selected option C: structural variety

beats.text: Exactly. Rows, stacks, groups. Different pictures, same math underneath.

params.description: Equation from M9 appears: 6 × 5 = 30
Equation from M10 appears: ☐ = 3 × 10

beats.text: You came in knowing expressions and products. Now you write complete equations. You know the equals sign means same value as. You find unknowns in any position. And you see equal groups everywhere: bags, rows, stacks, groups.

params.description: M9 equation fades out
M10 equation fades out
Simple rectangular arrangement of dots appears: 3 rows of 4 dots, not labeled as array

beats.text: Next time, you'll see equal groups arranged in a new way.

params.description: Rows highlight briefly, then fade
Columns highlight briefly

beats.text: Rows AND columns. You'll have a lot to notice.

elements.description: Rectangular arrangement of dots (3 rows of 4), columns highlighted
